_comment: Test bench for task5_speller_api, v0.2 — Lyra-aware. Each case fixes a BCI prefix plus a synthetic Lyra runtime state, and asserts that the top prediction is a Lyra vocabulary word (command verb, entity noun, state word, or category) rather than a generic English word. LLM outputs vary, so cases carry `acceptable_top_3` for tolerance. See DESIGN_NOTES.md §7 for the integration design this file tests against.
_harness_contract: Runner sets per-case lyra_state and calls predict_words(prefix, context, sentence, lyra_context=lyra_state). PASS if result[0] == expected_top_word OR result[0] in acceptable_top_3. Cases with `lyra_state: null` are prose-mode (no vocabulary injection) and must still return a reasonable general-English word.

lyra_vocabulary._note: Mirror of task5_speller_api/lyra_vocab.py (to be added — see DESIGN_NOTES.md §7). If one changes, change both. Source of truth in Lyra: backend/app/api/v1/endpoints/tasks.py (verbs), backend/app/db/models.py (states), backend/app/db/seed.py (categories).

cases.name: sc → schedule (planning mode, user anchor example)
cases.context: user is composing a command in Lyra; no active stopwatch; several tasks planned for today
cases.rationale: Prefix `sc` matches Lyra's canonical `schedule` verb. Bias must beat generic English candidates (`school`, `scene`).

cases.name: t → task (planning mode, user anchor example)
cases.context: user is issuing a command in Lyra; no active stopwatch
cases.rationale: Single-letter `t` is maximally ambiguous in English (the/to/this/that). Bias to Lyra's primary entity noun `task` whenever the caller is in command-mode.

cases.name: st → start (pre-stopwatch, user anchor example)
cases.context: user is about to begin a planned task; no stopwatch running yet
cases.rationale: Classic context-sensitive case. Same prefix maps to `start` when no stopwatch is running and to `stop` when one is (see next case).

cases.name: st → stop (executing, demonstrates state-dependent bias)
cases.context: stopwatch is currently running on a fitness task
cases.rationale: Identical prefix to previous case, different lyra_state → different top word. This is the core of 'task-governed' prediction.

cases.name: re → reschedule (planning)
cases.rationale: Prefix `re` resolves to `resume` when paused, `reschedule` when planning. Hot-verb list disambiguates.

cases.name: sk → skip (planning, deciding not to do a task)
cases.context: user is deciding to abandon a planned task without starting it

cases.name: de → delete (planning)
cases.context: user wants to remove a task entirely

cases.name: un → undo (just made a mistake)
cases.context: user just created or deleted a task and wants to revert; undo window is 30s

cases.name: me → meeting (category noun, composing a task title)
cases.context: user is typing the title of a new task; office setting
cases.rationale: Prose-mode within a command: the speller is composing a task title. Category bias (`meeting` is a Lyra category) should still beat generic English.

cases.name: fi → fitness (category noun, composing)
cases.context: user is titling a new morning task

cases.name: empty prefix → article/question-starter (no Lyra state)
cases.rationale: Mohamed's prompt explicitly handles empty prefix + empty sentence → article or question word. No Lyra bias applies.

cases.name: pure prose with sentence context (no Lyra bias)
cases.context: composing a personal email, outside Lyra command flow
cases.rationale: When the caller is composing prose (not issuing a Lyra command), speller must still return good general English. `lyra_state=null` signals prose-mode.

cases.name: noisy prefix (non-alpha) → graceful degraded mode
cases.context: BCI misfire; prefix contains a non-alphabetic character
cases.rationale: Relaxed validation (see INTEGRATION.md §4): non-alpha does not raise. Expect hot-verb fallback when the alpha suffix `s` matches a hot verb's stem.
